title: Market failure: externalities and the environment
summary: This lesson explores market failure arising from externalities, where the actions of producers or consumers have spillover effects on third parties not involved in the transaction. We will analyse negative and positive externalities of both production and consumption, illustrate them using diagrams, and evaluate various government policies designed to correct these market failures and improve social welfare, with a particular focus on environmental issues.
learningObjectives: Explain the concepts of externalities as a cause of market failure, distinguishing between positive/negative and production/consumption externalities.
Draw and interpret diagrams for all four types of externalities, identifying market equilibrium, social optimum, and welfare loss.
Analyse and evaluate government policies to correct market failure, such as indirect taxes, subsidies, carbon taxes, and tradable permits.
Apply economic concepts to real-world environmental issues like pollution and climate change.

simpleExplanation.title: The Neighbour's Messy Garden
simpleExplanation.summary: Market failure happens when the price of something doesn't reflect its true cost or benefit to everyone. Externalities are these hidden costs or benefits that 'spill over' onto people not involved in the original activity.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine your neighbour decides to stop looking after their garden. It becomes overgrown and attracts pests. This is a 'negative externality' for you – your property value might decrease and you have to deal with the pests, even though you didn't participate in the 'transaction' of them neglecting their garden. Conversely, if they created a beautiful garden, you'd get the 'positive externality' of a lovely view and pleasant smells without paying for it. The market for gardening services doesn't account for your pain or pleasure.
steps: First, identify the private market equilibrium. This is where the private costs of the producer (supply) meet the private benefits of the consumer (demand), ignoring any external effects.
Next, identify the externality. Is it a hidden cost (negative) or a hidden benefit (positive)? This external effect is added to the private cost/benefit to find the true social cost/benefit.
Then, determine the socially optimal equilibrium. This is where the marginal social cost (MSC) equals the marginal social benefit (MSB), representing the best outcome for society as a whole.
Finally, analyse the welfare loss. The difference between the market outcome and the social optimum creates a deadweight loss, representing the total value lost to society due to the market failure.

flashcards.back: A situation where the free market fails to allocate resources efficiently, leading to a net loss of social welfare. The price mechanism fails to account for all costs and benefits.
A cost or benefit arising from an economic transaction that affects a third party who is not directly involved in the transaction. Also known as a spillover effect.
When the production of a good or service creates external costs that harm third parties. Example: A factory polluting a river. Here, Marginal Social Cost (MSC) > Marginal Private Cost (MPC).

When the consumption of a good or service creates external costs that harm third parties. Example: Second-hand smoke from cigarettes. Here, Marginal Social Benefit (MSB) < Marginal Private Benefit (MPB).
When the production of a good or service creates external benefits for third parties. Example: A firm's research and development that other firms can use. Here, MSC < MPC.
When the consumption of a good or service creates external benefits for third parties. Example: Getting a vaccination protects others as well as yourself. Here, MSB > MPB.
The cost to the producer of producing one additional unit of a good. This is represented by the market supply curve.
The total cost to society of producing one additional unit of a good. It is calculated as MSC = MPC + external cost.
The total benefit to society of consuming one additional unit of a good. It is calculated as MSB = MPB + external benefit.
The loss of economic efficiency and social welfare that occurs when the market is not at the socially optimal equilibrium. It is represented by a triangle on the externality diagram.
A tax levied on any market activity that generates negative externalities. The tax is intended to correct an inefficient market outcome by being set equal to the external marginal cost.
A market-based solution to negative externalities, particularly pollution. A government sets a cap on the total level of pollution, issues permits to firms, and allows them to trade these permits.

Forgetting to label axes (Price, Quantity), all curves (MPC, MSC, MPB, MSB), equilibrium points (e.g., Qp, Pp, Qso, Pso), and the welfare loss triangle will cost you significant marks. Be precise and consistent.

faq.q: Why does the free market overproduce goods with negative externalities?
faq.a: Producers and consumers make decisions based on their private costs and benefits. With a negative externality, the true cost to society (MSC) is higher than the private cost (MPC) faced by the producer. Because the producer doesn't have to pay for the external costs (like pollution), the price of the good is artificially low, leading to a higher quantity being produced and consumed than is optimal for society.

faq.q: What is the key difference in the diagrams for a negative externality of production versus consumption?
faq.a: In a negative externality of production (e.g., factory pollution), the external cost is on the supply side. This means there are two supply curves: S=MPC and S1=MSC, with the MSC curve above the MPC curve. In a negative externality of consumption (e.g., smoking), the external cost is on the demand side. This means there are two demand curves: D=MPB and D1=MSB, with the MSB curve below the MPB curve.

faq.q: Are carbon taxes or tradable permits a better solution for climate change?
faq.a: Both are market-based solutions with different strengths. A carbon tax provides price certainty (firms know the cost of emitting a tonne of CO2) but quantity uncertainty (the total reduction in emissions is not guaranteed). Tradable permits provide quantity certainty (the 'cap' guarantees a maximum level of emissions) but price uncertainty (the price of permits can be volatile). The 'better' solution is a major debate among economists and depends on policy goals, political feasibility, and the specific context of the economy.

faq.q: Why is the welfare loss triangle shaped the way it is?
faq.a: The welfare loss triangle represents the cumulative loss to society from each unit produced beyond the social optimum (for negative externalities) or not produced up to the social optimum (for positive externalities). The height of the triangle at any given quantity between Qp and Qso represents the difference between the marginal social cost and marginal social benefit for that specific unit. Summing up this loss for all the 'inefficient' units creates the triangle.

sections.content: Welcome to the study of externalities, a cornerstone of market failure. In a perfect world, the 'invisible hand' of the market guides self-interest to promote the good of society. But what happens when it doesn't? This lesson delves into the spillover effects of our economic choices, from the pollution caused by industry to the societal benefits of education. We will learn how to identify, diagram, and propose solutions for these failures, which is essential for understanding modern economic policy debates, especially concerning the environment.
The Language of Externalities: Private vs. Social

At the heart of externalities is a divergence between private and social costs and benefits. Economic agents—consumers and producers—make decisions based on their private costs and benefits. However, when an externality exists, there is an additional cost or benefit imposed on society that is not reflected in the market price.

items: **Private Costs/Benefits:** The costs/benefits directly incurred by the individuals involved in a transaction.
**External Costs/Benefits:** The spillover costs/benefits imposed on third parties.
**Social Costs/Benefits:** The total costs/benefits to society as a whole. They are the sum of private and external costs/benefits.

sections.content: Marginal Social Cost (MSC) = Marginal Private Cost (MPC) + Marginal External Cost (MEC)
Marginal Social Benefit (MSB) = Marginal Private Benefit (MPB) + Marginal External Benefit (MEB)

Market failure occurs because the free market operates at the point where MPB = MPC, ignoring the external components. The socially efficient or 'allocatively efficient' level of output is where MSB = MSC.
Negative Externalities: The Costs We Don't Pay For

Negative externalities occur when the social cost of an activity is greater than the private cost. This leads to over-production or over-consumption of the good in a free market, resulting in a welfare loss to society. A classic example is industrial pollution.

items: **Negative Externality of Production:** MSC > MPC. The market over-produces the good (Qp > Qso). Examples: Air pollution from factories, noise pollution from an airport.
**Negative Externality of Consumption:** MSB < MPB. The market over-consumes the good (Qp > Qso). Examples: Smoking in public places, traffic congestion from driving.

sections.content: When drawing negative externality diagrams, the welfare loss triangle always points towards the socially optimal quantity (Qso). The base of the triangle is the vertical distance between the two relevant curves (e.g., MSC and MPC) at the market quantity (Qp). This shows the size of the external cost per unit at that output level.

sections.question:
A steel plant's production process pollutes a nearby river. The market for steel has a demand function $P = 150 - Q$ and a supply (MPC) function $P = 30 + Q$. The external cost of pollution is estimated to be a constant £20 per unit of steel produced. 

1. Find the market equilibrium price and quantity.
2. Determine the socially optimal quantity and the price at that quantity.
3. Calculate the size of the Pigouvian tax needed to correct this externality.
4. Show the value of the welfare loss.
sections.solution:
1. **Market Equilibrium (MPB = MPC):**
   Set demand equal to supply: $150 - Q = 30 + Q$  
   $120 = 2Q \implies Q_p = 60$ units. 
   Substitute Q back into either equation: $P_p = 150 - 60 = £90$. 
   *Market equilibrium is at Q=60 units, P=£90.*

2. **Socially Optimal Equilibrium (MSB = MSC):**
   First, find the MSC function. MSC = MPC + External Cost. 
   The MPC curve is $P = 30 + Q$. So, $MSC = (30 + Q) + 20 = 50 + Q$. 
   The MSB is the same as MPB (demand curve), as there's no consumption externality: $MSB = 150 - Q$. 
   Set MSB = MSC: $150 - Q = 50 + Q$ 
   $100 = 2Q \implies Q_{so} = 50$ units. 
   The price at this quantity on the demand curve is $P_{so} = 150 - 50 = £100$. 
   *Socially optimal equilibrium is at Q=50 units, P=£100.*

3. **Pigouvian Tax:**
   The tax should equal the marginal external cost at the socially optimal quantity. Here, the external cost is a constant £20 per unit. 
   *The required Pigouvian tax is £20 per unit.*

4. **Welfare Loss:**
   The welfare loss is the area of the triangle between Qso and Qp, bounded by the MSC and MSB curves. 
   Base of triangle = $Q_p - Q_{so} = 60 - 50 = 10$ units. 
   Height of triangle = Marginal External Cost = $£20$. (Or MSC - MPC at Qp: $(50+60) - (30+60) = 110 - 90 = £20$). 
   Area = $0.5 \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = 0.5 \times 10 \times 20 = £100$. 
   *The welfare loss due to overproduction is £100.*

sections.content: Positive Externalities: The Benefits We Don't Get Paid For

Positive externalities exist when the social benefit of an activity exceeds the private benefit. This leads to the under-production or under-consumption of the good in a free market, as producers and consumers do not account for the benefits bestowed upon others. This also results in a potential welfare gain that is not realised.

items: **Positive Externality of Production:** MSC < MPC. The market under-produces the good (Qp < Qso). Example: A beekeeper whose bees pollinate a nearby apple orchard.
**Positive Externality of Consumption:** MSB > MPB. The market under-consumes the good (Qp < Qso). Examples: Higher education, vaccinations.

sections.question:
The market for university education has a demand (MPB) function $P = 40 - 0.5Q$ and a supply (MPC=MSC) function $P = 10 + 0.5Q$. Prices are in thousands of pounds (£'000) and quantity is in thousands of students. It is estimated that university education provides an external benefit to society (e.g., a more productive workforce) represented by the function MEB = 10. 

1. Find the market equilibrium quantity and price.
2. Determine the socially optimal quantity of students.
3. Calculate the per-student subsidy required to reach the social optimum.
4. Show the value of the potential welfare gain.
sections.solution:
1. **Market Equilibrium (MPB = MPC):**
   Set demand equal to supply: $40 - 0.5Q = 10 + 0.5Q$ 
   $30 = Q$. So, $Q_p = 30$ thousand students. 
   Substitute Q back: $P_p = 10 + 0.5(30) = 10 + 15 = £25$ thousand. 
   *Market equilibrium is 30,000 students at a price of £25,000.*

2. **Socially Optimal Equilibrium (MSB = MSC):**
   First, find the MSB function. MSB = MPB + MEB. 
   $MSB = (40 - 0.5Q) + 10 = 50 - 0.5Q$. 
   The MSC is the same as MPC, as there's no production externality: $MSC = 10 + 0.5Q$. 
   Set MSB = MSC: $50 - 0.5Q = 10 + 0.5Q$ 
   $40 = Q$. So, $Q_{so} = 40$ thousand students. 
   *The socially optimal quantity is 40,000 students.*

3. **Optimal Subsidy:**
   The subsidy should equal the marginal external benefit at the socially optimal quantity. The MEB is a constant £10 thousand. 
   *The required subsidy is £10,000 per student.*
   This subsidy would shift the MPB curve up by 10 to become the MSB curve, or shift the MPC curve down by 10, achieving Qso.

4. **Potential Welfare Gain:**
   This is the welfare loss from under-consumption, which can be gained by moving to Qso. It's the triangle between Qp and Qso, bounded by the MSB and MSC curves. 
   Base of triangle = $Q_{so} - Q_p = 40 - 30 = 10$ thousand students. 
   Height of triangle = Marginal External Benefit = $£10$ thousand. 
   Area = $0.5 \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = 0.5 \times 10 \times 10 = £50$ million. 
   *The potential welfare gain from correcting the market failure is £50 million.*

sections.content: Governments can use several policies to address externalities and move the market towards the social optimum. These are broadly categorized as market-based policies (taxes, subsidies, tradable permits) and command-and-control policies (regulations, direct provision).

items: **Pigouvian Taxes:** Increase MPC to align with MSC. Pro: Internalises the externality, raises government revenue. Con: Difficult to quantify the exact value of the external cost, can be politically unpopular.

**Subsidies:** Decrease MPC or increase MPB to encourage production/consumption. Pro: Increases output of merit goods. Con: High cost to government (opportunity cost), difficult to quantify external benefit.

**Tradable Permits:** Sets a cap on pollution and creates a market for permits. Pro: Guarantees a certain level of pollution reduction, creates an incentive for firms to invest in green technology. Con: Can be expensive to monitor and enforce, initial allocation of permits can be politically difficult.

**Regulation:** Legal limits or bans (e.g., emission standards for cars, ban on smoking in public). Pro: Simple to understand and can be very effective if enforced. Con: No incentive for firms to go beyond the minimum standard, can stifle innovation, high enforcement costs.

sections.content: For evaluation questions (e.g., 'Discuss the best way to solve...'), don't just list pros and cons. Use the P-P-C-C-L-E framework: Prioritise arguments, consider short-run vs. long-run effects (Perspectives), weigh Costs vs. Benefits, acknowledge Constraints (e.g., information gaps, political feasibility), and consider Local vs. national impacts (or different stakeholders). A strong conclusion will make a justified judgement based on this evaluation.
